verses.text: For he felt that it would be a terrible thing, after really gaining the victory, to be vanquished by the tongue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In consequence, he so won upon them by his long-suffering, that, when time had lent its aid to his judgment, they exchanged their annoyance for admiration, and knelt before him to ask his pardon, in shame for their previous conduct, and flinging away their hatred, submitted to him as their patriarch, lawgiver, and judge.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: From the same zeal proceeded his opposition to the heretics, when, with the aid of the Emperor’s impiety, they made their expedition, in the hope of overpowering us also, and adding us to the number of the others whom they had, in almost all cases, succeeded in enslaving.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For in this he afforded us no slight assistance, both in himself, and by hounding us on like well-bred dogs against these most savage beasts, through his training in piety.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: On one point I blame you both, and pray do not take amiss my plainspeaking, if I should annoy you by expressing the cause of my pain.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When I was disgusted at the evils of life, and longing, if anyone of our day has longed, for solitude, and eager, as speedily as possible, to escape to some haven of safety, from the surge and dust of public life, it was you who, somehow or other seized and gave me up by the noble title of the priesthood to this base and treacherous mart of souls.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In consequence, evils have already befallen me, and others are yet to be anticipated.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For past experience renders a man somewhat distrustful of the future, in spite of the better suggestions of reason to the contrary.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Another of his excellences I must not leave unnoticed.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In general, he was a man of great endurance, and superior to his robe of flesh: but during the pain of his last sickness, a serious addition to the risks and burdens of old age, his weakness was common to him and all other men; but this fitting sequel to the other marvels, so far from being common, was peculiarly his own.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: He was at no time free from the anguish of pain, but often in the day, sometimes in the hour, his only relief was the liturgy, to which the pain yielded, as if to an edict of banishment.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: At last, after a life of almost a hundred years, exceeding David’s limit of our age, forty-five of these, the average life of man, having been spent in the priesthood, he brought it to a close in a good old age.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And in what manner?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: With the words and forms of prayer, leaving behind no trace of vice, and many recollections of virtue.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The reverence felt for him was thus greater than falls to the lot of man, both on the lips and in the hearts of all.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Nor is it easy to find anyone who recollects him, and does not, as the Scripture says, lay his hand upon his mouth and salute his memory.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Such was his life, and such its completion and perfection.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And since some living memorial of his munificence ought to be left behind, what other is required than this temple, which he reared for God and for us, with very little contribution from the people in addition to the expenditure of his private fortune?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: An exploit which should not be buried in silence, since in size it is superior to most others, in beauty absolutely to all.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It surrounds itself with eight regular equilaterals, and is raised aloft by the beauty of two stories of pillars and porticos, while the statues placed upon them are true to the life; its vault flashes down upon us from above, and it dazzles our eyes with abundant sources of light on every side, being indeed the dwelling-place of light.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is surrounded by excrescent equiangular ambulatories of most splendid material, with a wide area in the midst, while its doors and vestibules shed around it the lustre of their gracefulness, and offer from a distance their welcome to those who are drawing nigh.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I have not yet mentioned the external ornament, the beauty and size of the squared and dove-tailed stonework, whether it be of marble in the bases and capitals, which divide the angles, or from our own quarries, which are in no wise inferior to those abroad; nor of the belts of many shapes and colours, projecting or inlaid from the foundation to the roof-tree, which robs the spectator by limiting his view.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: How could anyone with due brevity describe a work which cost so much time and toil and skill: or will it suffice to say that amid all the works, private and public, which adorn other cities, this has of itself been able to secure us celebrity among the majority of mankind?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: When for such a temple a priest was needed, he also at his own expense provided one, whether worthy of the temple or no, it is not for me to say.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And when sacrifices were required, he supplied them also, in the misfortunes of his son, and his patience under trials, that God might receive at his hands a reasonable whole burnt offering and spiritual priesthood, to be honourably consumed, instead of the sacrifice of the Law.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What sayest thou, my father?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is this sufficient, and dost thou find an ample recompense for all thy toils, which thou didst undergo for my learning, in this eulogy of farewell or of entombment?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And dost thou, as of old, impose silence on my tongue, and bid me stop in due time, and so avoid excess?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Or dost thou require some addition?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I know thou bidst me cease, for I have said enough.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet suffer me to add this.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Make known to us where thou art in glory, and the light which encircles thee, and receive into the same abode thy partner soon to follow thee, and the children whom thou hadst laid to rest before thee, and me also, after no further, or but a slight addition to the ills of this life: and before reaching that abode receive me in this sweet stone, which thou didst erect for both of us, to the honour even here of thy consecrated namesake, and excuse me from the care both of the people which I have already resigned, and of that which for thy sake I have since accepted: and mayest thou guide and free from peril, as I earnestly entreat, the whole flock and all the clergy, whose father thou art said to be, but especially him who was overpowered by thy paternal and spiritual coercion, so that he may not entirely consider that act of tyranny obnoxious to blame.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And what do you think of us, O judge of my words and motions?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If we have spoken adequately, and to the satisfaction of your desire, confirm it by your decision, and we accept it: for your decision is entirely the decision of God.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But if it falls far short of his glory and of your hope, my ally is not far to seek.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let fall thy voice, which is awaited by his merits like a seasonable shower.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And indeed he has upon you the highest claims, those of a pastor upon a pastor and of a father upon his son in grace.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What wonder if he, who has through your voice thundered throughout the world, should himself have some enjoyment of it?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What more is needed?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Only to unite with our spiritual Sarah, the consort and fellow-traveller through life of our great father Abraham, in the last Christian offices.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The nature of God, my mother, is not the same as that of men; indeed, to speak generally, the nature of divine things is not the same as that of earthly things.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: They possess unchangeableness and immortality, and absolute being with its consequences, for sure are the properties of things sure.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But how is it with what is ours?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: It is in a state of flux and corruption, constantly undergoing some fresh change.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Life and death, as they are called, apparently so different, are in a sense resolved into, and successive to, each other.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For the one takes its rise from the corruption which is our mother, runs its course through the corruption which is the displacement of all that is present, and comes to an end in the corruption which is the dissolution of this life; while the other, which is able to set us free from the ills of this life, and oftentimes translates us to the life above, is not in my opinion accurately called death, and is more dreadful in name than in reality; so that we are in danger of irrationally being afraid of what is not fearful, and courting as preferable what we really ought to fear.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There is one life, to look to life.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: There is one death, sin, for it is the destruction of the soul.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: But all else, of which some are proud, is a dream-vision, making sport of realities, and a series of phantasms which lead the soul astray.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: If this be our condition, mother, we shall neither be proud of life, nor greatly hurt, by death.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What grievance can we find in being transferred hence to the true life?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: In being freed from the vicissitudes, the agitation, the disgust, and all the vile tribute we must pay to this life, to find ourselves, amid stable things, which know no flux, while as lesser lights, we circle round the great light?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Does the sense of separation cause you pain?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let hope cheer you.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Is widowhood grievous to you?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Yet it is not so to him.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And what is the good of love, if it gives itself easy things, and assigns the more difficult to its neighbour?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: And why should it be grievous at all, to one who is soon to pass away?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: The appointed day is at hand, the pain will not last long.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let us not, by ignoble reasonings, make a burden of things which are really light.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: We have endured a great loss—because the privilege we enjoyed was great.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Loss is common to all, such a privilege to few.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Let us rise superior to the one thought by the consolation of the other.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For it is more reasonable, that that which is better should win the day.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: You have borne, in a most brave, Christian spirit, the loss of children, who were still in their prime and qualified for life; bear also the laying aside of his aged body by one who was weary of life, although his vigor of mind preserved for him his senses unimpaired.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Do you want some one to care for you?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Where is your Isaac, whom he left behind for you, to take his place in all respects?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Ask of him small things, the support of his hand and service, and requite him with greater things, a mother’s blessing and prayers, and the consequent freedom.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: Are you vexed at being admonished?

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: I praise you for it.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: For you have admonished many whom your long life has brought under your notice.

verses.book: Select Orations of Saint Gregory Nazianzen.
verses.text: What I have said can have no application to you, who are so truly wise; but let it be a general medicine of consolation for mourners, so that they may know that they are mortals following mortals to the grave.
